In the next hour we're going to be focusing on something that can bring tremendous value to you whether you're a business owner or a copywriter. So the goal here for copywriters on this call is to add something to your toolbox that gives you the opportunity and the power to multiply your income. If you're a business owner as we're about to see, these tools can multiply your sales revenues every year by many, many times over. So having said that, let's get going. The Make-Peace Marketing multiplier is just a fancy name for the strategies I use to market to a house file. And when you're thinking about a customer file, a list of your active customers, and we'll define active in a moment because it can be different for different kinds of companies. But when you're creating your strategy to sell things the second, third, fourth, fifth time and on down the line to your active customers, there are only three things that you really have to do to maximize revenues from your house file. The first one is to systematically multiply the number of times each of those customers buys from you. The second one is to systematically multiply the amount each customer spends with you each time. And the third thing is to systematically increase the time during which each customer buys from you. Some of the companies you'll run into will contact customers maybe once a month or once a quarter with an offer. And so systematically multiplying the number of times each customer buys from you basically means systematically multiplying the number of times you contact the customer. A lot of people will tell you, people who haven't tried this, who haven't gotten involved with this, will tell you, oh, we don't want to bother our customers. We don't want to contact them too often because it'll turn them off. The fact of the matter is people love to spend money. And if you're contacting them with good offers on good products that bring value to their lives, then they want to hear from you. We actually ran tests on this at Blanchard where we took, when I first went to work for Blanchard selling gold and silver coins, in the 80s, he was contacting his customer file once a month. I immediately doubled it to twice a month. And over the next three months or so, we went to four times a month. Within a year, we were contacting the list every single week. And we were slicing and dicing the list, so we had up to 17 promotions going out at one time. After a while of doing that, we pulled the file and we also looked at our numbers and we found that the increase in contacts actually was increasing response rate, was increasing average unit of sale, and was also causing our customers to stay with us longer. So anyone that tells you that contacting customers regularly or frequently will burn out the file probably has no data to back them up. So anyway, these are the three steps that we're going to go with today. And we're going to talk about how to do these things and how to do it in ways that actually increase response and increase average sale with every promotion. Let's take a look at what this means when we increase each one of these three things and why we call this the marketing multiplier. If you increase the number of purchases a customer makes per year, you multiply the annual sales and customer lifetime value. And on this table here, we can see a company that, let's say for just purposes of our discussion today, they're doing two or three or four promotions per year. They're getting each, the average customer maybe makes one purchase a year with an average sale of $100. That means the total annual revenue per customer is only $100. If the customer stays with us for years, his lifetime value then is $400. And you can see how that lifetime value and annual revenues increase as the number of purchases per year increase. This is not rocket science. This is not brain science or rocket surgery. This is just very simple arithmetic. Then let's see what happens when we take the next step. Here we've settled on, we've gotten to the point where our average customer is making three purchases per year. Now we want to increase the average sale of these customers. And look what happens to our annual revenue. Remember we started with $100 annual revenue per customer. Now we're up to $300, $450, $600 depending on how high we're able to raise this average sale. And look what's happened here to our lifetime value. It's gone from $400 to $1200 at the bottom run and all the way up to $4200 at the top run. So there's a multiplying effect going on here. It's not a one for one increase. The next step is pretty impressive too. This is where the rubber meets the road in terms of the value of the company. If you go to a company owner and his company is doing $3 million in sales a year for example, because he's got 30,000 customers each one of which is buying $100 worth of product a year. And you tell him that we can take you to and only staying with him for years and we can take you to these kinds of lifetime values. Remember the original lifetime value I think was what $1600 or something like that. Now we're up to $5,400 if we can keep him with us for nine years. The next chart really brings us into focus. Or in our little example here, we had revenues per customer of $100. In a company with 30,000 customers, that's $3 million a year in sales. After working to increase average sale, frequency of sale, and lifetime value, we're now up to $600 per customer revenue per year with 30,000 customers and we now have an $18 million company. That's a six-fold increase. We only increased buying frequency by 200%. We increased average sale by 100%. And that multiplied our annual revenues by 600%. Incremental changes mean a lot. But look what happens with our lifetime value. Our average lifetime value was $400. Remember they bought $100 worth of product per year and they were only buying for four years. At 30,000 customers, that's $12 million in total value per customer, total lifetime value. But now by increasing our lifetime value to $4,200, we now have a $126 million company. So again, incremental changes, 200% increase in buying frequency, 100% increase in average order, and extending average lifetime just 75% gave us a 950% increase in total customer lifetime value. And it increased the value of the company exponentially. Now think about what that means to us. Now some of the people on this call are business owners. Some of us are copywriters. For the copywriters in the group, you all know that I'm constantly nagging you to be more than copywriters, be strategists, be marketing consultants, acquire skills that allow you to bring more value to your customers. If you're a business owner in this thing, the benefit to you is clear. You just took your sales up six-fold in a year. If you're a copywriter or a marketing consultant, and let's say you're getting 10% of sales on house file promotions, you get $1.8 million a year. That's good income. Now imagine setting one company up on this kind of a program, getting your $1.8 million a year going, and then moving on, now you've got a template, you've already done it once, and getting another company or another two or three companies on this, you can see how your income will really skyrocket. And again, this has been a huge source of income to me over the years, being able to do this kind of thing. Now a lot of company owners, you're going to talk to are going to balk at 10% of sales. That's fine. You could take a slightly larger percent of the increase in sales. You know how much you're going to be able to increase that client's company. If you go in there, he's got a great product. Everything is going for him, but he doesn't have this kind of a house file strategy working. He has no clue as to what you're about to do for him. So usually, if you go in and say, look, I'll do the work for free on the front end. All I want is 10% of sales. A lot of company owners will jump at the chance. Let me make one other point here. When you're writing acquisition programs, let's say you're writing a mag-a-log to get new customers. It's going to take you a month to write it if you're good and if you're fast. It's going to take another two to three weeks to get it through graphics, get it designed. It's going to take another two or three weeks to get the thing printed. It's going to take another two weeks or so for them to know whether it's a winner or not. Then it's going to take another three to four weeks for them to turn it around and get it back in the mail. So you can often be looking at four to six months before the second mailing of your great mag-a-log happens. And if you took an advance against royalties on the front end, even the second mailing may not be large enough to generate royalties for you. So it could literally be eight and nine months before royalties from a customer acquisition promo start flowing to you. But when you're doing house file promotions, you're writing stuff today that's going to go out to that house file tomorrow. There's not going to be small incremental tests rolling out to bigger tests unless you're working with a company with a gigantic database, a gigantic customer file. So what you're writing today goes into the mail or into email tomorrow or next week or the week after instead of months from now and can generate royalties right away. So in that kind of a situation, I don't charge advances. I'm doing a lot of work with Weiss research right now. I don't charge them anything up front for my promotions to their customer file because I write it today. It goes out by email tomorrow. And I know that on the 15th of next month, I'm going to get a royalty check for it. So all that just to say you can afford not to work on advances and just take straight 10%. But if a client company balks at that, says, well, I don't know, that's a lot of money. There are other things that you can offer. You can ask for 5% of sales and a stock option. The 5% of sales will get you income now to pay your bills and the 5% stock option gives you a stake in the company. And some later today, we're going to do a session just on deal making. But there's a lot I want to say about structuring a stock option deal. Another way to do it would be to ask for 5% of sales now and a 5% bonus at the end of the year. The customer or client only sees the 5% he's going to have to fork over now. Failing that, you can ask for a monthly retainer to cover your bills and a stock option or performance bonus quarterly or at the end of the year. The point here is that there's almost always a way to make a deal. And it just takes a little bit of creativity and a little bit of patience. And you can get to the kind of deal that will get you at the end of the day, a minimum of 10% of the sales that you produce. After all, that's only fair. If you were a commissioned face-to-face salesman, you'd probably be making that kind of money anyway. And if you're writing customer acquisition promotions and getting $50 a thousand in the mail on a promotion that costs $500 a thousand and the customer is mailing to break even, you're getting 10% of sales on customer acquisition promos anyway. So it's a very fair deal for all parties involved. And in our example, we showed that you got the company to $18 million in sales. He's got to pay you $1.8 million of that. That still leaves a huge increase for the company owner. So it's a win-win all the way around. By the way, smart marketing companies will pay more than 10%. Dan Rosenthal used to, I don't know if he's still doing it, but Dan Rosenthal used to structure deals where he got 30% of sales because he brought something to the table that the company couldn't possibly do internally. So value what you can do, value what you're going to learn today. And it really is a key to dramatically increased income for you as a copywriter. And if you're a business owner and you want to understand this, but you don't necessarily want to do it yourself, finding a partner who knows how to do these things for you is worth its weight in gold. All right, so then we just established that if you can do these three things, that's really the key to exponential growth. So what does it take to do those things? Well, obviously, it takes superior products. It takes products that will make your customers want to come back to you over and over again. It takes great offers, offers that customers find irresistible. It takes great sales copy, which is one of the things that all of you either are already producing or are well on your way to being able to produce. It takes top-notch customer service. When the customer ordered, he felt loved. He felt cared for. The product came quickly. If there was a problem, it was handled quickly so that the entire experience was positive. But after that first purchase, the purchase that made him a customer in the first place, it takes more personal contact with these people. Now they're part of your family. Now they're one of your friends. And so you don't speak to them in the same way that you speak to a prospect on a cold mailing list that you just rented. You know things about this customer that you didn't know about that prospect, and we'll get into that in a moment. But being able to access that information is key to increasing the number of purchase, increasing response rate, average sale, and therefore number of purchases and annual revenues from each customer. It requires more frequent contact, as we also already discussed. One way to get more orders is to increase your response rates, which you can do through better copy, better offers, more personal contact. The other way is through more frequent contact, making those contacts enjoyable for the customer. I've actually had situations where customers wrote to us and asked us where their promotion piece was that month because they missed it. If you do it right, we'll cover that a little bit today, but we're going to do it in depth later in a webinar on how to talk to a customer. But more frequent contact will also result in increased sales. Varied types of contact is a key to making those more frequent contacts palatable and enjoyable for the customer. And then great bonding strategies. We're going to cover all of these in principle today. The first step in setting up a program for a client company is really to define what an active customer is. At most companies that I work with, we define an active customer as somebody who's purchased a product from the company within the last 12 months. That isn't carved in stone anywhere. The fact of the matter is if you're working with a company, say a book club company, they have a different product cycle. They may consider a customer dead in the water if he hasn't ordered in 90 days. For most companies, and certainly with the gold coin companies I've worked with, the newsletter publishers I've worked with, the health product buyers I've worked with, the financial newsletter and health newsletter companies I've worked with, if a customer hasn't bought a product from you within 12 months, he pretty much expires. He's handled in a completely different way. He's still contacted by the customer, but if he hasn't responded in 12 months, what does that mean? Well, it means he's not likely or as likely as a regular customer to respond to your next promotion. What does that mean? It means you can't spend as much money to talk to him as you can to a customer that buys from you every 30 or 60 or 90 days. They're handled entirely differently. Step one, though, is if you're going into a company is to figure out what you're going to define as an active customer. Maybe somebody who's bought it three months, six months, 12 months is pretty much the norm for the companies I work with. Now, each of those customers' information is stored on a customer file, and that's just simply a piece of software that keeps database records in it. Some of those customers are going to be more valuable to the company than others. You're going to have non-buying leads and inquires. Every company has people who have written them letters or called and asked questions about the product but didn't buy. That's the lowest level on our database here. The next level is the expired customers, people who had bought in the past but who have not purchased in 12 months or longer. At every company you'll work with, almost every company, this group here, the expired customers is going to be the biggest file he's got because he's been in business for years. He's got, let's say, at Phillips, for example, Phillips Publishing. They had eight or 10 financial newsletters. They were publishing them for years. Each one had tens of thousands of subscribers. Over the years, tens of thousands of subscribers allowed their subscriptions to laps. They have this large group of expired customers. This is a fantastic asset for a company to have because whenever you mail a customer acquisition package, this file here, your expired customer file, is going to respond much higher than cold rental files of people who don't know you. But then you can see the progression here. You're going to have low frequency, low amount buyers on your file who are active. From here up, it's active customers. You're going to have medium frequency, low amount buyers. You're going to have medium frequency, medium amount buyers and so forth until you get to marketing nirvana here. These are your high frequency, high amount buyers. They may buy from you one time a month, two times a month, three times a month, and each purchase is at the maximum amount that you ask for in your promotion. Your mission then is to systematically move buyers up through the ranks to get as many of these guys to this level as possible, as many of these guys as possible to this level and so forth, which would by definition reduce the number of names at this end of the scale, therefore increasing revenues and company value. Okay, I think that's pretty clear. Let's move on. Promotions crafted to upgrade selected file segments. Remember in the previous slide, what we're trying to do every month is upgrade portions of the file to make the file more responsive and more valuable to increase customer lifetime value. Okay, to increase customer lifetime value. Promotions crafted to do that, to upgrade these file segments, require that you be able to select portions of the file by certain key criteria. Those criteria quite often, usually will include these things, sometimes others, and we'll look at that in depth in a moment. At age, you want to be able to go to your file and say, okay, well, I notice that the people who are between 40 and 50 are not buying our product at the same rate as people between 50 and 60. So I may want to create a campaign to change that, okay, to identify what the problem is and then to address it, therefore moving all of these people up to the next level. Or maybe it's men versus women. Okay? I may want to select a group of people based on how long they've been on my file. Inner circle campaigns are one way to do this. Hey, let's take everyone that's been with us for five years or longer and do a campaign to them where they offer them everything we produce at some ungodly high price. We'll talk about that in a moment. We've done that successfully with financial products. Product preferences. These guys in a supplement company, all of these guys love heart products, but none of them ever buys an antioxidant product. Well, let's change that. That would require a campaign maybe that shows how antioxidants promote heart health just as well as the dedicated heart products that we've offered in the past. One frequency is another selector that you may want to use. We'll look at all of these actually from here on down in more detail right now. So now, if you have that data, what can you do? Well, here's one way to use it. You could say you could create a promotion and then you could say, well, let's mail this to just our mail customers. Only the people that are 50 years or older. Only people who have been with us 12 months or more. Only the people who have bought with us at least three times a year. And whose average order is over $200. And who spent a total of $1,000 or more with us. I used this kind of a campaign at Blancherton Company when we were introducing some platinum coins, the world's first platinum coins that were just being released by the Swiss Mint. And I was able to say, okay, guys, look, I want a list of everyone that's been with me for three years or more who buys from us at least six times a year, whose average order is over $3,000, and who spent at least $50,000 with us. And give them that kind of fine detail. They came back with a mailing list of a couple of thousand people. Now, I knew that these were our prime customers. These people love us to death. And they love our products to death. And so I was able to confidently pack up these $3,500 platinum proof sets and send one each to each customer on approval and just ask them to return it if he didn't want it. We didn't have a single customer refuse to pay us for the set or to actually, actually we sold out the entire vintage just with that one promotion. Every customer that received it either returned the set and declined to order or kept the set and ordered more. And that's the power of knowing your customers well enough to do this kind of segmentation. So the idea here is of being able to match product offers with the customers who are most likely to respond, multiplies response and revenues from every segment of your file. It automatically upgrades customers' recency and total amount spent with your company. Gives you enormous power. Another way to use this kind of data would be in a promotion where we want to target groups that we want to upgrade. In the first case, we targeted groups that we knew were most likely to buy this product. In this kind of a promotion, you target them on the basis of, well, you know, this person has bought three times per year average for the last four years and we want to upgrade him to four time purchase. And he's right there now. He's bought three times this year. So if we can push him over the line and break him through that mental barrier, that emotional barrier and make that additional fourth purchase, we will have upgraded him. Most importantly, it comes to price point. This guy has never spent more than $399 with us on a particular purchase. If we can get him to spend $425 on this next promotion, we will have broken him through that emotional barrier and gotten him into the $400 range. That brings enormous value to the file. So that's the second way of using this kind of data. Now the heart of it all is customer record. This is a record that exists in software, database software that your client has, or if he doesn't have, he's going to need for you to be able to work your magic with him. This is just a sample of the kind of information that would exist on a customer record. By the way, just to make this easy, you're probably aware of Microsoft Excel. It's just a simple spreadsheet program. A lot of these database programs are little more than glorified spreadsheet programs that are set up in a way that allow them to store this information and then crunch the numbers and manipulate the information. But on this one here, this is Mr. Fred Flintstone. We have his address. We know where he lives, his city, his state, his zip code. Let's just look at this for a moment. Let's say in the online model, our model is to generate an easy subscriber first and then to turn him into a customer. So when he opted into our easing, we knew his first name was Fred and we knew his email address. So all of these other fields were empty at that point. We didn't know his middle initial, his last name, his address, any of this other stuff. But since then, Fred has bought something from us. And when he made his first purchase, we learned his middle initial, his last name, his address, his zip code, city, state, zip code, his phone number, most likely, depending on how our shopping cart was set up. So we learned all of this about him. So the minute he purchases, we know this material from about here up. Right? Now the next step is to start fleshing out the rest of what we know about the guy. We'll talk about his personal stuff in a minute. But we also know now that we acquired him on this date. We know that the source was a package that Clayton Makepeace wrote. That's what MP means. And it was package number seven that Clayton Makepeace wrote. We know that it was a direct mail package, not an online promotion. We know that he bought Safe Money Report, a newsletter. We know that he paid $99. And we know that he ordered by telephone. All Fred seems to like the telephone. He had an order form or response device, but he chose to call instead. The activity code we're going to cover in depth here next. But then the next part of the customer record, you have personal information, you have where he came from. One thing I didn't put on here that is also a very good idea after the source, you know, the creative, the direct mail piece. The other piece of information that is very helpful to know is what mailing list you found his name on. And that would typically be a code that you could look up because there are so many mailing lists that you're using. You probably wouldn't want to put the whole name in. But you'd have a code that would tell you what mailing list he came from. That gives you additional information in terms of what he bought in the past and so forth should you want to check that out. All right. Then we have the activity code, which is also called an RFA code. We're going to get into depth on that. And then you have his activity history. And for every time he purchases from you, you're going to record when he bought the source of the purchase. This is, in this case, is an email blast number 234 where you sent him an email and he responded to that and bought the product promoted in the email. The contact mode in this case was email. He bought Real Wealth Report, which is another newsletter. He paid full boat $189. And once again, he called his order in rather than to buy it on the shopping cart. So this is a basic customer record. It gives you a lot of information about the customer. Now, today, in these days, software is advanced to the point where you could typically tell somebody, OK, I want everybody who is bought, who came on my file, who became a customer prior to 229. And that person would, and your database people, your IT people, would be able to generate a list for you. You just give them plain English instructions. And they can generate that list for you. A lot of companies that don't have that kind of sophistication rely very heavily on activity codes or RFA codes. These were first developed decades ago, mostly by the fundraising industry, where they needed to know how active their donors were and how much they gave on average. And I learned about them from fundraisers and began adapting them in 1982 to product sale companies because they gave me amazing control over how I wanted to segment my customer file each month for promotions. And so let's, we'll take a look at those in a minute. I want to go back to this. If any of you have read Harvey McKay's books, you know about the McKay 66. Harvey's, Harvey, Harvey's a character. Harvey spoke at our conference last year and he was a hoot. But he came up with this idea that every time we have contact with a customer, we're going to learn something more about that customer because knowing something about, knowing more about the customer makes us closer to them, makes it easier to make the next sale. Now Harvey worked in face-to-face sales. His sales reps sold envelopes. He owned an envelope company up in Minneapolis. So if a salesman came back with a sale but didn't come back with an additional piece of information about the client, his commission wasn't as large. And Harvey enforced that. And so over time, he was able to build up a database of, on his customers where he knew so much about them that they felt more like friends than customers. And the company and the sales rep felt more like a friend than a sales rep. And I've been adapting the same thing in direct response marketing. I mean, if it works face-to-face, why wouldn't it also work when you're communicating to large groups of people? And so apply them a case 66. Identify other things that if you know them about your customer, you can use them in your sales copy to that customer. Here are just a few ideas. You can find out what his educational level was, where he went to school, what he majored in in college, what his hobbies are. This is particularly interesting. If he has a religious preference, what his spouse's name is. There's nothing better on a customer file than being able to send out a reminder that your wife's birthday is coming up. spouse email address. So I really shouldn't be writing you, but Fred really needs a little push from behind. He's forgotten to schedule your vacation this year. Children's names to use in a field in a personalized communication and birthdays. Depending on what your product is, you may find a completely different set of extended customer information is helpful. But now this gives back to copywriting, and if the copywriter knows that he has this kind of information available to him when addressing a particular file segment, then it's going to have a big impact on how he writes the copy because he can personalize a letter to mention some of these things are key on them. It also gives you other ways that you can segment the file. You could decide, for example, to only go to people whose hobby is bowling, or only people who have children, or only people who have daughters, or whatever. So it gives you tremendous power over your file. Now, the way to get that information is threefold. The first thing that I do when I'm working with a new customer, a new client, is to pull the file. There's a lot that I want to know about the file, and I'm willing to bribe his customers to answer my little survey. And it'll typically be just four or five questions, and at least one or two of those questions will be carefully designed to add a key piece of information to his customer file that I can use in later promotions. Also it's very important to task the telephone customer service reps to engage customers in conversation to flesh out each customer's record with every telephone contact. That's really the most natural time to do it. And I have a client that has 30 or 35 telephone sales reps, and every single time someone calls in, it needs to be like you're calling a friend. And so it's chatty, it's friendly, and you find out where they're going on vacation. You find out what their hobbies are, and you get to know more about their lives, and it immediately gets added to their customer file. You see, the key here isn't a nuts and bolts one. The key here is to create this overall feeling of family, of warmth. We care about you not just because you give us money, we care about you as a person. And gathering this information allows you to create those kinds of promotions. Now we talked about the activity code or the RFA code. In companies that don't have more advanced data processing and IT departments, and who are basically maintaining their mailing list themselves on a spreadsheet or whatever, you may want to use the RFA code. And all it is is a series of digits. Here's one, for example, that tells you five crucial things about each active customer. These are the things that you're going to use most often to segment the file in order to upgrade the customer, and also to select customers who are most likely to respond to a given offer. The first one is the recency code. In this case, that's a G, and that's time since his last order. He may have ordered three or six or nine months ago, for example. The second one is a frequency code, and that's here. As the average number of orders he's placed per year during his lifetime. As we already discussed, that's an important piece of information to have. When he gets to, let's say he orders three times per year on average, and it's June, and you find that he's ordered three times this year, is he going to be dead to you for the rest of the year, or are you going to do a campaign to up his average number of orders per year in the months ahead? The next one is the amount one code, and that's the amount of the average purchase over the customer's lifetime. That's self-explanatory. Amount two is the amount of the largest purchase to date. You may have had a whole lot of little 49 or $29 orders, but one time he bought $129 product from you. It tells you what his mental or emotional ceiling has been so far in terms of buying from your company. Again, very important number because you'll want to upgrade that amount systematically. Then five is the total purchases to date, amount three. That allows you to select groups of people who have bought the most from your company. The strongest customers, and to make special offers to them. We'll look at what a couple of those kinds of offers might be in a moment. Again, here's recency. You can see this guy is a G. That means he's bought within the last 181 to 210 days. This is just to show you how the codes are often set up. Each letter of the alphabet has a date range. The reason that they use letters typically instead of numbers here, you could use a 30 here, but that's two digits. Rather than take up the room on the database for the extra digit, they find using alpha codes would save them that. This is really a holdover from the old days when computer storage was very expensive. At any rate, here you can see how recency is. This guy is a G. Therefore, his last order was within the last 181 to 210 days, something around seven months. Frequency code is a C. That means that he averages three orders per year. Not one is the average purchase size. His is between $100 and $199. Let me just say, if you're setting this up for a client, you get to decide what these are. What these ranges are. You may decide, for example, because a client's smallest purchase price is $49 that an A customer would be a $0 to $49 customer or a $1 to $49 customer or whatever. This is part of setting up the client's house file marketing program. I just made all this up. This is arbitrary. Again, this is driven more by what the price points are of your client's products. In this case, anyway, his average purchase size is between $199 and $100. You may also decide that you want these to be finer in terms of the ranges. You may want the smaller ranges to give you more control. Mount two is the largest purchase date. Same deal. Mount two, this guy's largest purchase was somewhere in this range. Mount three is total purchases to date. This guy has spent a total of somewhere between this amount. This range is typically set up by where you see the break points on your customer file. You can adjust them from time to time. You may find that the ranges are larger or smaller, whatever. But if you think your way through it, you'll get it right. This slide got a little fouled up. But anyway, this is what you do with that information. The customer we just looked at. His last order was placed seven months ago. You can upgrade his buying frequency at this point. He places an average of three orders a year. You can get him to buy four. His average purchase size is between $100 and $199. You can send a promotion to him to get him to buy a product that costs more than $199. His largest purchase to date is between $200 and $399. You can do the same thing here to break that barrier. His total purchase date is in this area. Everything you do to him really is aimed at upgrading him in this area. So that's the use of RFA codes. Okay. Once you have your database set up, you're going to want your client to produce a report for you. And that report is going to have counts for each of the items in your recency, frequency, and amount codes. So this is the recency count form. This is what the report would look like. And it would simply tell you then how many people fall into each one of these recency categories. So this many people have bought in the last 30 days. This many people in the last 60 days. And when you look at this, you start noticing things. For example, you notice that if you don't do something pretty quick, you've got 71,000 total members and total customers. You notice if you don't do something quick, you're going to lose 8,000 of them in the next 30 days, another 5,400 in the next 30 days, and another 5,000 in the next 30 days. All together, that's 18,000 people who are no longer going to be active customers if you don't do something to activate them. So immediately, what does that say? Oh my God, I've got to mount some kind of a special campaign to get as many of these people as possible to make a purchase right away. What does that mean? Well, it may mean a special offer. It may mean a special customer appreciation thing. Anniversary with a company. You've been with us eight months or nine months, and I just want to thank you for being a great customer. Here's a special discount on product XYZ. The next thing that you'll notice, you'll notice some lumps in here. You'll find that you've got this big rabbit going through the snake here at 211 to 240 days. Now that guy, this number is going to be here next month, here the month after that, and so forth, until you get, until they fall off the file. So there may be something you want to do with these people. You can also notice that the number of people coming in in the last 90 days, or who have made a purchase the last 90 days, is somewhat smaller than the number of people who are about to fall off the file. So you'll learn a lot about this. If you take this form, and on this column right here, you make this January's quantity, and then February's quantity, and March, and it's updated every single month, then you begin seeing trends in each one of these categories going horizontally. And that will show you if your purchase quantity, the last 30 days, was higher or lower, and you'll be able to see how each of these categories are changing, and you'll be able to see opportunities and challenges on the file, and head them off at the pass or capitalize on them, that you'd never even have known existed without this report. Now this is just the recency report. You'll also have one for frequency that will show the same information, and one for each of the amount categories. It gives you enormous power in terms of creating the strategy for promoting to that house file. All right, let's look at some rules for promoting to a house file. These, like everything with me, rules tend to be rules of thumb, but at least consider them. They have worked very well for me. Rule number one, when I'm working with a new client, and especially with copywriters, who are writing for me for that client, we have four crucial goals that we want to accomplish. In every promotion we send to the house file, the first one is to strengthen the bond between the customer and the company. If you go in with bombastic sales copy, over promising, threatening, insulting, just to make the sale, you may make the sale this month, but you may have lost the customer forever. If you send a customer an abusive number of promotions, especially a customer who has asked not to be sent a large number of promotions, you may make a sale today, but you're spending moral capital. At some point down the road, this is going to adversely affect his lifetime value to your company. He'll either begin ordering less or he'll drop out altogether. On the other hand, if each promotion has as a main objective to make the customer feel closer to the company, more trusting in the company, and more in love with your products or services, now you've taken a big step towards extending customer lifetime value. We saw what that does in terms of increasing the value of the company. The next objective with every promotion is to acquire a new piece of information about the customer. We just talked about that because that gives you enormous marketing power down the road. The third thing is to upgrade the customer in some way, in terms of frequency, recency, or amount. Finally, of course, to produce maximum revenues. The revenues comes last. Again, if you produce maximum revenues, but you make the customer eager not to hear from you again, you've blown it. It's really important to say that because there are so many people who are so intent on this month's sales that they're unwilling to leave any money on the table. Sometimes leaving a little money on or even giving away a free gift with no ulterior motive is the way to make sure that customer is going to order next month and the month after and the month after that. It's just like any relationship. At some point, the customer adds up the pluses and minuses and decides whether or not he's going to want to continue it. You want to always come out on the positive side of that equation. Every contact with a customer is an opportunity to bond, sell, and gather information. A lot of companies, especially the ones that are run by bean counters, are looking for opportunities not to communicate with customers. They're looking for opportunities to cut costs by reducing the number of things that you send to customers. Like most of the things bean counters do, it's idiotic and upside down. Instead what you need to be looking for is ways, more ways to contact the customer, more excuses to write to him or to call him or to send him an email. I forget who it was. I saw on Michelle Fortin's blog, somebody had come across one of my clients and they had bought a product from him. In the welcome kit, in the package with his first product, he got a free calendar. He got a bunch of coupons for discounts on future purchases. He got a very nice, warm, personal letter from the owner. He got like 20 or 30 pieces of paper in that package. Bean counter would say, hey, your outgoing packages are too expensive. The material in that package bonds people to us and they're much more likely to come back and make a secondary purchase 30, 60, 90 days down the road because they get all of that nice, warm, fuzzy stuff and added value they didn't expect. You look for opportunities to contact the customer and to spend money through contacting customers. You have your normal outgoing promotions. We're going to look at what a typical monthly promotion might be like in a moment. Receipt and thank you letters. A lot of people forget about this, but if somebody buys something from you and it's going to be a couple of days before you ship, why not send them a first class letter or an email that thanks them for their purchase and then in the thing, either ask them to fill out a short survey or poll or has a little stuffer for an additional product that they can add to their order at a favorable price. These always pay for themselves. If they're done right. Our shipment and packaging, we just talked about that. You're sending the package anyway. It costs you very little in terms of postage or shipping costs to include other materials that bring value to the customer and therefore bond him to the company. Customer inquiries and complaints. Again, the telephone sales force is extremely important here. Working with them and finding out how they handle these things is valuable on so many levels. Number one, you can find out what the complaints are. That's as valuable as testimonials really in terms of crafting future promotions. Scripting those people so that every time that they talk to a customer, they get a piece of information that you can use, whether it's a complain or an inquire or whatever. Scripting them so they give away something free. If somebody calls in to complain that maybe an order was mixed up or something, have a list of three or four things that you can send the person to apologize. Gee, I'm sorry we messed up that order. We never do that kind of thing. Just please accept this as a token of my sincere regret and have a free special report or a free gift of some kind. It costs you very little. Look for opportunities to do opinion polls and surveys. People love sharing their opinions. Sometimes even if the answers to the poll are not necessarily helpful to you, just the fact that you demonstrated your concern for the customer by asking for their opinion causes bonding to take place. One of the problems that people are having right now in the product first vitamin supplement industry, product first means you send out a promotion selling a supplement and you just make a direct supplement sale. You're not selling a health newsletter first and then they buy something that the doctor recommends in the newsletter. So on product first promotions you've got a product, you send out a whole bunch of direct mail pieces and you get a whole bunch of new customers. One of the problems that companies that do this are having is that their customers don't know who the company is. They buy a lot of health products. They buy their vitamins at the grocery store. They buy their minerals at the drug store. They buy something from healthy directions, something else from health resources, something else from nature's way. They're not bonded to the company. They may not even know who the company is. They know what the product is that they're looking forward to receiving. The company has no visibility in their mind. Going to them with a promotion where you ask their opinion in a poll or a survey raises the visibility of your company and increases the chances that that customer is going to remember you, remember the company when you go back to them with another promotion. Typically customer file promotions to those kinds of customer lists yield very low response rates. The reason is because they're not aware of the company. They know they ordered the product, but they're not bonding. This is a good way to do that. Value added gifts. Again, there's nothing that has the impact of ordering a product and getting a couple of unadvertised bonuses in the product. Also we love calendars. We love to do a calendar with the owner of the company and his whole family, his mom and dad and his kids and everybody else on the cover. Every month on the calendar there's a coupon for a discount on one of the client's products. Obviously, it needs to be something that the customer is going to want to keep. It's got to be big. It's got to be beautiful. Something they're going to want to tack up on the wall or stick on their refrigerator. That kind of thing has a lot of benefit to you. Not only will the coupons in the calendar generate more sales than you normally get, but being able to see the company owner and his family and he becomes a real person, part of your family, not just a salesman who wants to get his hand in your wallet. Company newsletters are also great. We'll look at how we've used those in a moment. Then events, like this one. Something like this is great. It takes a day or two to put together a PowerPoint for a webinar and then an hour and a half, in this case, I guess, to actually do the webinar. It has tremendous value for your customers. The cost is very nominal, but it brings a lot of value to people's lives and creates a very strong bond with the customer. Rule three, be willing to spend at least as much to keep a customer as you're willing to pay to get a customer. I was talking to Drayton Bird last week about this. He's doing database management. He's doing just this kind of thing right now for Richard Branson at Virgin Atlantic. He's dealing with very savvy business people in multi-billion dollar companies. He has the same battle that the companies are not structured to keep customers. They're structured to go out and get customers, but if a customer is leaving the fold, they don't have a plan for that and they don't allocate a budget for that. It only makes sense to be willing to spend at least as much to keep a customer as you're willing to pay to get one. Think about it. It's all right here on this slide. Response rates are typically eight to 10 times higher when you're selling to somebody who's bought from you before as it is when you're selling to cold prospects, rented lists. Average sale is typically higher too, sometimes twice as high. Getting new customers requires capital risk. To do a mailing of a million pieces costs a half a million dollars. You put that money out there. What if a new war starts? What if there's a terrorist event? What if the stock market crashes? What if OJ offs another Y for X, Y? All of a sudden people are glued to their TVs and they're not reading your mail and your response falls through the floor and that whole half a million dollars goes down the drain. I've seen it happen. That money is at risk. It doesn't matter how well proven the promotion is. I've seen a promotion go from 100% of cost ROI down to 20% just because of some unforeseen national news event. Getting new customers involves significant capital risk. The higher response in higher average sale to your customer file gets you coverage. Even if the worst happens, you're not going to lose your money on the mailing. Those customers are very important. Smart marketers are willing to pay at least four times more to keep one. I just have one slide to put that in perspective. If your average customer is with you for seven years, which by the way is about the average that we find over time, seven year lifetime. If you're doing better than that, you're fantastic. If you're doing less than that, that's kind of a good thing. If your client's doing less than that, it's a good thing because you get to be the hero that gets him to seven years or maybe even a little more. If your average customer is with you for seven years and spends an average of $500 a year, using that average customer will cost you $3,500. How much are you willing to spend of that $3,500 to make him happy? Give him a refund, let him keep the product. I'd be willing to call. For $3,500, I'd be willing to spend three minutes on the phone to apologize to a customer. What's it going to take? Rule four, first impressions mean a lot. This goes back to customer acquisition as well. Let's just think this through. Step one, when you're contacting a prospect, he's not yet a customer, is the time to start the bonding process. Again, bonding equals customer lifetime value. Bonding equals longevity, and as long as he's with you, he's going to be buying, so bonding equals lifetime value. That's the goal of all of this. When you're doing your initial promotion, you want personal messaging. People don't bond with companies, they bond with people. You have personal messaging from the president of the company or from your figurehead or advocate. You establish the company position as being superior in some way to the competition. You create trust. A big part of that, really, in the promotion, we would be doing anyway in terms of credibility devices, testimonials, and other things. Trust extends beyond just the promotion piece. It also extends to step two, which is the actual buying experience. To have the people who answer the phone, not to be some of these slugs that I've heard when I call in to order something from an infomercial or something, or from some other kind of promotion. They need to be warm, they need to be friendly, they need to be trained to handle the customer and to treat them like a king. They need not to be abusive. This is one of those areas where you have an opportunity to make a sale and make sure, at the same time, that customer will never buy another blessed thing from you as long as he lives. If you've ever responded to an infomercial on TV, you know what I'm talking about. You call in to buy that $19 product and that customer service rep beats the holy crap out of you until you buy five other things and then he wants to charge you $35 for FedEx shipping. That kind of thing basically guarantees that that customer is never going to come back to you again. You set up the service, the telephone reps in a way that they handle that customer with kid gloves and make him feel like he's joining a family. You turn your product around fast. There's a personal thank you letter from the president. If you don't want to send it separately as a first class letter, which I would do, fine. Stick it in the product package, put it in a nice envelope. Personalize the envelope. Advertise the letter. Deliver an unadvertised bonus. This next one is really important. If a new customer is coming on your file for this first time, don't immediately throw him into the normal promotional cycle that you're doing to your customer file. Create a new customer experience. It can be a week long, two weeks long. I've done them up to 90 days long where every new customer that comes in goes through a very careful process whereby he's given tremendous value, he's bonded with the company, and he's prepared prior to being put into the regular promotional cycle that you have at your company. But just as a quickie, I have a client, for example, that has PPC campaigns on the web that draw you in if you're searching for advice on international investing, and another PPC campaign that draws you in if you're looking, searching for information or advice on currency investing. Well, we're creating one experience for the people who are interested in foreign investing, and a completely different experience for the ones that are interested, who have told us that what they're interested in is currency investing. And the goal of both of these programs is to stay with the subject that they've told us they're interested in, and to have a product ladder that moves them from new opt-in to the e-zine to their first $29 product offered to their $49 to their $99. And then by the time they get to that point, they're ready to join the file where we're offering products at a range and price from $1,000 to $25,000. So think through that process and think how your customer came to you, what he told you about his interest by responding to your first offer to him, and how you can then continue to deliver or address that interest or that concern, that fear, frustration, or desire in that first period of time on file. Okay. There are two kinds of promotions. Typically, now we're getting down to planning your monthly promotion strategy. Okay. There are two kinds of promotions generally that you'll do to a file. The first type are horizontal promotions. And we call them that because they cut across the file based on what a particular shared qualifications of a particular segment. That for example, a horizontal promotion might target a section of the file based on their recency frequency or one of the amount categories we've identified. You might say, I want to mail a promotion this month to everybody who has a birthday this month or everyone who has an anniversary this month or all the guys or all the gals or all the people between 30 and 40 or 40 and 50 or all the people in Dayton, Ohio or all the people who have been with me a year or more or all the people who placed a recent order. Then there are vertical promotions that target the entire file and are triggered by shared events. Imagine you've got a big file. This is cutting layers off the cake. Okay. This one is drilling down through the cake and going to the whole file. Holidays and red letter days, Christmas, Easter, New Year's, Hanukkah, Kwanzaa, 4th of July, holidays, red letter days, breaking news events. We get a lot of this in the financial area. On Friday, President Bush and Ben Bernanke both went to Great Pains to say that they're going to spend as much of my money as is necessary to bail out lenders who made risky real estate loans. That is a breaking news event that to a financial file is very important. That promotion would go to the entire file. New product announcements. If you have a new product and if the price point is appropriate for your entire file, we'll talk about that in a second, then you may want to go vertical with that and go to the entire file. Sometimes, like for example, we just launched two products on currencies. One of them is a $2,500 product and the other one is a $900 product. The reason we did that was the $2,500, they're very different products, but they both focus on the same concept, currencies. We're taking the people who have bought more with us, our best recency frequency and amount and they're receiving the promotions for the $2,500 service and we're taking the rest of the file and they're getting the promotion for the lower price service. That's possible too if you have that kind of price variation. Two kinds of promotions horizontal and vertical. The point is you've got a month laying out before you like a great banquet that you're about to take part in. You get to decide and you've got this wonderful customer file sitting there with all this money in their pockets and every one of them is a bundle of needs for the products that you have. The question is how do you get the right offers to the right people? How do you make sure that you do some upgrading on your file every month, that people are upgraded on basis of recency frequency and amount every single month? You lay out a schedule that might look something like this. Again, this is just to give you the concept you're going to have to do. You're going to have to think your way through this and it'll be different for every client that you work with. But this one is fairly typical. We're going to have an e-letter for this particular client and he will let us run ads in that e-letter. They go out that E-Zine goes out Monday through Saturday in the morning and people can order either by phone or the shopping cart. This is going to be a horizontal promotion. In other words, we're going to stratify the list and we're going to have higher price products for our higher price people and we're going to have lower price products for our lower price people. But we're going to stretch them a little bit and we're going to skew it so that we're offering $200 products to people whose largest purchase was $150 until now. So we're upgrading them by price. That's going to be our e-letter plan for this month. The products may change but that's the basic strategy. Then we're going to, in the afternoons every day except Sunday, we're going to send an email promo. Again, sales devices, the phone or shopping cart. When it's horizontal, we're going to stratify the file. These can be countdowns. They can be whatever you want them to be. But again, the point here is to be thinking about who's going to receive each message and making sure that they're being upgraded in some way. Then we have our print newsletter that goes out on the first Friday of the month. People can order from an insert in that newsletter by phone or by return mail. This is a print piece. If we want to get real fancy, we can make this vertical, I mean horizontal, and upgrade by price. Typically though, it would be, and this is wrong, typically it would be vertical. Everybody would receive the same offer. We would select a product at a price point that would be attractive to the greatest number of people on our file. Then on the second Friday of the month, we would do a standalone direct mail promo. This would be a personalized letter, a postcard, any kind of format we want to do. But it would be highly personal, and it would go to the entire file. Again, it could be horizontal or vertical depending on what we want to do, whether we want this to be an upgrade, whether we want to match our products to the people who are most likely to buy them, which is basically a revenue maximizer, or whether we can just do a vertical and just everyone gets the same offer. On the third Friday, everyone gets a mag-a-log, essentially, that we call a company magazine. It has a lot of value added editorial that makes people love it and want to read it. Some of the articles culminate in a product offer, and there are also ads for the product. We can have various products in that, and it's totally vertical. Everyone gets the same magazine. Then finally on the fourth Friday, we have another standalone promo. Again, a very different format from the one that they received two weeks early. This can be horizontal or vertical, either way, your call. In this case, I'm saying segment by the largest sale. It could be recency, frequency, whatever you want to upgrade on. There's another kind of promotional schedule you can look at, and that's when you're launching a new product. Again, just very quickly going through that. This is pretty self-explanatory, but basically, when you launch a new product, you want to have an introductory price. You want to have it, and the best discount they'll ever get is if they buy this product now, and you want to have a deadline when that offer is going to end. When you have a deadline, that means you can have a countdown to the deadline. You can be reminding people that if they don't order now, they only have three days left, two days left, one day left. Very effective. This is what one of those campaigns might look like. Again, you can go through it as self-explanatory, but you use all of the same devices. Now, you're multimedia. You're using e-zines, email promos, print newsletters, standalone DM. I'm really clear here. It can be a personal letter. It can be any size format. It can be anything you want it to. Because this is your customer list, it should be personal, as personal as possible. Anyway, in this case, everything is focused on the countdown and the new product, the countdown and the offer. Here's some tips to think about as you're designing these campaigns. First, in direct mail, it almost always pays to use first-class postage. That's for several reasons. Number one, believe it or not, there are people who notice that when they receive letters from you, they come first-class. You invested the time and money to send them a letter to go first-class with them. For the ones who are a little dull and don't notice it right away, I like to stick a big, old red stamp on the outside of the envelope that says first-class mail. But it says something about how you value them as a customer. It also gets you your buying data back faster. Instead of a two-week delivery time that you'd get with bulk mail, which slows down your entire campaign on your house file, you get delivery in three days. Some of your people get next-day delivery on the first class. Who are close by the mail center that you use. First-class on customer house file promotions is just really important because it gives you the kind of timing accuracy you need in order to have the number of customer contacts you need to conduct an optimal house file promotion campaign. There are two heavily personalizing communication with customers pays dividends. I don't mean just saying, dear Mr. Flintstone, at the beginning. Remember you have a customer record and you know a lot more about the guy. At the very least, you can say things like, I hope things are going great for you there in bedrock, or has it been a hot summer in bedrock, or how's old Wilma doing? This is where a great customer record really pays dividends. I told the story before of how this is outside the customer record. We wanted to know how many savings and loans and banks in each customer's area within a 30-mile radius of their house were in trouble. This is back during the savings loan crisis. We were able to find a database that told us that. All the database told us was which institutions were in trouble and where they were located. By zip code matching, we were able to say, hey, you know that savings loan down on the corner of such and such and so and so? Well, it's a big trouble. We were able to tell them a little bit about it. We did another one that you've probably seen where we matched a AAA report on gasoline prices and we were able to tell people that the gas station down on such and such an address in their town was charging this much for gas. Those kinds of promotions get a lot of attention and response rockets when you can get that kind of personalization into your promotions. Three, keeping a variety of formats going. We talked about that earlier in this call. But again, we've used postcards from France. We've used MAG-a-Logs. We've used envelopes that look like federal express envelopes. We've used envelopes that look like invoices, envelopes that look like checks. Constantly be changing the format. When you go to them with the same old look all the time, some people just say, well, I've seen this before and it gets trashed. They don't even open it. But keeping it interesting, keeping it lively, creating a fascinating tableau keeps response high no matter how many times you contact these people. Giving your prospects or your customers non-promotional gifts once, a quarter, twice a year or just before the holidays is also great. We talked about the unadvertised bonus, but this is more than that. Maybe they didn't even order, but they get something from you of value. It amazes me that so few people use coupons, for example. A discount coupon is just an absolutely wonderful way of saying, I love you to a customer. Send them a 10% discount as a just because gift in February for Valentine's Day. This is my love letter. You don't have to get sappy about it, but you can say, I've been thinking about all the things that I have to be grateful for, and that's when I thought of you, John. I just wanted to send you this discount. No promotion, no strings. Just here's a coupon. Use it anywhere you want. It's my way of saying thanks for everything you've done for me and for our company over the last year. You don't think the phones will ring when you send that out. You don't have to tell them what to do with it. They know how to use a coupon. Look for opportunities to bond in those kinds of ways. Pays dividends. Offering customer rewards, credits for purchases provides many more opportunities for profitable customer contacts. This takes some doing. It takes setting up the IT. It's some coordination. The airlines have frequent flyer miles for a reason. They know that it's going to bring you back. It brings you to the airline. The first place during the mile and brings you back again to build up your balances. The same thing is true if you're selling supplements or many other kinds of products. If somebody is buying something from you that they need five or six times a year, making sure they get it from you and not somebody else might be as easy as giving them a vested interest in your company. Every purchase they make is worth so much towards their next purchase or some other kind of rewards program. You might also try an inner circle offer. This is good for mature companies. I have one client where the client offers a bunch of different services and they range in prices all over the map from as little as $29 up to $5,000. We set up two inner circles for him. Again, one for our lower echelon buyers and one for our higher echelon buyers. The first one sells for $15,000 and the second one sells for $25,000. Once you join this, you get all of his services for life forever. The $25,000 service, you get everything the company produces for $15,000. You get everything he produces with his name on it as author. We've sold millions of dollars worth of those. Consider inner circle when you have a company that has a significant number of names at the top, significant number of high frequency, high average purchase names at the top, people who buy just about everything you offer them. It's time to seriously consider one of these inner circle things. Again, in another call, we'll talk about that in more detail. I'll show you how I do it. Here is the consultant's guide to getting going on setting up this kind of a program. Four, five simple steps. Number one, really identify what is a customer. We talked about that. An active customer, somebody who's ordered with the last 12 months in most cases. Make sure each record contains all valuable data on each customer that you have. I've gone into companies where they have some of the data on their database about a customer. Like for example, they'll have all of his transactional history, but they won't have any information on where he came from in the first place. Or they won't have information on there about any refund requests he's made. Go through the database records. That information was on paper somewhere in the company, and so we had to import it into the database. Make sure that every scrap of information you have on every customer is on the customer records. Number two, ask the question. You don't have to be a computer genius. I certainly am not. Ask the question. Go to whoever knows and say, hey, look, if I want to mail just to the 50-year-old guys who have bought within the last three months and spent more than $200, and over their lifetime spent more than $2,000, and if I ask you something that specific, can you do it? And if so, how long will it take? Chances are most people will say, yeah, we can do it. What they won't tell you is they would probably take them six weeks to figure that out. So if the software isn't set up to very quickly and efficiently be able to segment the file, you've got some work to do there. I have to say that when I went to work at Blanchard, they were in that kind of a situation. It took us six months to be able to get our first reports that told us what our average lifetime value was. It's worth the work. It's worth the time. You can still be promoting in the meantime to the house file, but getting this information up front is very important. Step number two, establish your benchmarks. This is before you appeared on the scene. You've got all of this data now, so now you're going to print some reports, and you're going to establish how many names in each RFA category, like the reports we looked at. You're going to look at the recency frequency amount for the average customer. That's very different. This tells you how many names are in each recency frequency and amount category. This tells you that across the entire company, the average recency of order is three months. The average frequency is six times a year. The average amount of purchase is X dollars. Average largest purchase among all our customers is this amount. Very different report, very important to know. By the way, one of the reasons that's important is because it establishes where you are today, and it allows you to set goals for each of these categories for tomorrow. See the longevity and lifetime value of the average customer. Again, this may take a while, but this is the holy grail. This is what everything else you're doing leads up to. Increasing longevity and increasing lifetime value. After having done that, now you have a clear picture of who your customers are, what your file looks like. The next step is to identify your objectives. Need to study those benchmark reports to determine the most pressing challenges and the biggest opportunities on those reports. We talked about looking at the cradle to the grave idea. When they're born, how they're handled, what could be done better there. The opportunity there is tremendous because a lot of new customers become non-customers, although you don't know it in those first 30 days because of the way they're handled. Look at the beginning and end of their lifetimes first. Then look in between to identify particular groups on the file by recency frequency and amount that seem to be presenting you with the greatest opportunities. This will also really help you in terms of product development and pricing for obvious reasons. If you find that your customers are 10 times more likely to buy a $49 product than they are a $300 product, then obviously you're going to make more money with a $49 product. You may find the opposite is true, but look for those kinds of opportunities on the file. Then create your promotion plan. Create spacing between each customer contact. Now in email, daily contact is just fine. Even twice a day if the contacts are very different is okay. Any more than that, you're going to get a lot of complaints and opt-outs. In the mail though, weekly is certainly fine. I've gone as often as three times a week and not seen any erosion at all in customer longevity. Actually again, the more visible you are to the, you know, the old saying, out of sight, out of mind. If you're constantly in their face, you're going to be top of mind for them. If you're not there, they're not going to be thinking about you. If you are there, then you're going to find that your response rates rise. In some cases, your average sale as well. But anyway, create your promotion plan and make sure that you have specialized promotions every month designed to upgrade weaker file segments, capitalize on stronger ones, and maximize revenue from the overall file. Then it's time to execute. That's when you get back to copyrighting. We'll take a couple of minutes to kind of wrap things up. Yeah, Jeff says that obviously a big limiting factor is the ability of the company. Let me talk about that a little bit. Jeff says that a limiting factor is the ability of the company to keep ginning up new products that are good. There are a lot of limiting factors of the companies that you're going to work with. Let's just talk about a few of those. How do you know that a company, a particular company, would be a good candidate for you to get into this kind of business with, to go in and set up house file marketing? Number one, there needs to be a trust level. There need to be people that you can trust to tell you the truth about what their revenues are. I receive sales reports from my clients four times a day. I know minute by minute almost, hour by hour certainly, during the working day. How well my promotions are working. I have a long enough relationship with this client that I know I can trust them. Trust is very important. One of the worst ways of time you'll ever get into is crawling in bed with somebody that is just flighty, not somebody that is going to end up messing with you and costing you money. It's so disappointing when that happens. Fortunately, I haven't had that happen a lot of times in my life. I think a lot of it was luck and some of it was just that I tend to be very picky when choosing clients. Another qualification company has to have in order to be a good fit for this kind of a deal is that the company has to want to grow aggressively. The company owner has got to want or be willing to invest in infrastructure to support what you're going to do. I once told a client that I feel like I'm a 4,000 horsepower dragster motor that somebody just strapped onto a roller skate. The company didn't have a marketing department that could support the kind of promotions that we needed to do. It takes qualified personnel to get all of this material to the customers, to stratify the file, to do all of the selects, and to get things moving. The company owner that you're going to do business with needs to be somebody that's willing to do that kind of investment in his own company. The people at the company, when you go to talk to them, you need to make sure that they feel open and excited about what you all are about to do together. If you get the feeling that they are guarding their turf and they really don't want you there, then that's a major problem that you should discuss with a company owner. Because there's not a lot you can do. I had an experience once where I spent six weeks at a client's offices reorganizing his company for him. We structured reports, or reporting system. We structured everything from the ground up, company structure, reporting structure, everything. To create just a kick butt online marketing department. The minute I left, his employees started going to the owner, and well, I don't really want to do this report. It would just take too long. I don't want to report just so and so. The owner caved on almost every point. In the end result, I gave up six weeks with my family to do this work with a client and nothing was implemented. You need to have a level of confidence that the work you're going to be doing with the client is going to be used. That's important. Yes, Jeff, the ability to create products goes in with all of that. Okay, guys. Well, I want to thank you for your time today. Either I've boggled your minds, or you're so busy taking notes that you don't have time to ask more questions. Thanks for everything. Take care.
